FINDING THE COMPLEMENT OF AN ELLIPTIC CURVE
INSIDE A JACOBIAN
ANDREA GALLESE, DAVIDE LOMBARDO, FRANCESCO NACCARATO,
AND UMBERTO ZANNIER
Abstract. This note gives a simple algorithm for the following effectivity problem:
given a genus 2 curve X together with a nonconstant map π : X →E to an elliptic
curve, determine an elliptic curve E′ and a map π′ : X →E′ independent of π.
Equivalently, we compute the complementary elliptic factor in the decomposition of
Jac(X) up to isogeny. While the problem has been studied extensively, and more
general ones have been solved by deep and powerful techniques, we are not aware of
a reference for the simple explicit procedure described here.
1. Introduction
A natural problem in the explicit geometry of abelian varieties is to determine, effec-
tively, a Poincaré decomposition of a given abelian variety A, that is, to describe A as
the product (up to isogeny) of simple abelian varieties. Closely related is the problem
of deciding whether two given abelian varieties are isogenous. Even for elliptic curves,
such questions can already be delicate, although in many cases non-isogeny is easy to
establish, and effective criteria in the CM case are classical.
Effectivity in problems of this form is in general highly nontrivial. Foundational work
of Masser and Wüstholz [16, 17] gives a powerful and general framework, in particular
over number fields. These results have had many important applications, but they rely
on deep methods and are usually not well suited for explicit computations in special
cases. Presumably, the same circle of ideas should also yield a complete answer over any
field finitely generated over Q. Over function fields, one expects simpler and more direct
arguments, but we are not aware of an explicit treatment of this case in the literature.
Recently, independent motivations led Gallese and Naccarato to the following
special case of the problem of computing an isogeny decomposition:
Problem 1.1. Given a curve X of genus 2 with a rational nonconstant map π : X →E
to an elliptic curve E, find an independent map π′ : X →E′ to an elliptic curve E′.
Remark 1.2. Here independent may be defined in several equivalent ways: for instance,
by requiring that the image of (π, π′) : X →E × E′ is not contained in a translate of
a proper algebraic subgroup. This is automatic if π′ is nonconstant and E, E′ are not
isogenous. We also note that the pair E, E′ is unique up to isogeny.
Such a map π′ does in fact exist by the classical theory: if J denotes the Jacobian of a
smooth model of X, then J is isogenous to a product J ∼E × E′ of elliptic curves, and
the composition X →J →E′ gives the desired map. Our objective here is to make E′
and π′ explicit. Our aim is not to pursue generality, but rather to isolate in this setting a
very simple and concrete procedure for recovering the complementary elliptic factor and
the corresponding map.
We shall briefly recall below some of the motivations that led Gallese and Nac-
carato to consider this problem from an effective point of view. For the moment, we
only note that, natural as the question may seem, we have not been able to find in the
literature an explicit treatment in the form considered here; moreover, when asking col-
leagues about it, we were not pointed to a reference. Of course, related aspects of genus-2
2020 Mathematics Subject Classification. Primary: 14H40, 14Q20. Secondary: 11G05, 14K02.
Key words and phrases. Split Jacobians, elliptic curves, genus 2 curves, effective algorithms, Prym
varieties, Poincaré decomposition.
1
arXiv:2606.02429v1  [math.NT]  1 Jun 2026
2
GALLESE, LOMBARDO, NACCARATO, AND ZANNIER
curves with split Jacobians and maps to elliptic curves have been studied before: see, for
instance, [10], [5], [24], and [11]. In contrast to the present work, where a map X →E
is assumed to be given, the algorithmic problem of finding such a map (when it exists)
is considered in [14].
Since in the special genus-2 setting we found a very simple method, which also appears
to be computationally efficient, we felt it worthwhile to record it. We would be surprised
if the underlying principle were genuinely new, so the basic idea is probably already
familiar to some experts, but an explicit account may still be of use to others.
In the sequel we first describe the method.
We then collect several remarks and
questions suggested by the construction, together with a few motivating examples, and
finally we briefly discuss related problems and possible generalizations.
2. The method
We assume that we are given a map π : X →E, with X, E, and π all defined over
a ground field K. We think of K as a computable field of characteristic different from
2, namely a field finitely generated over its prime field (say Q), presented by finitely
many explicit generators and algebraic relations. In practice, we assume that X and
E are given by explicit equations, for instance as plane or space curves. We will take
finite and computable extensions of the ground field K as necessary (for example, to fix
a K-rational base point on X) without further comment.
To keep the note as brief as possible, we shall not dwell on routine technical details,
which can be supplied or modified without difficulty. For example, we will sometimes
assume that X is smooth and projective, although for actual computations it may be
convenient to work with a singular or affine model.
Notation 2.1. We suppose that X is given by the affine equation
X : y2 = F(x)
where F ∈K[x] has degree 5 or 6, and that E is given by the Weierstrass equation
E : w2 = f(z),
where f(z) ∈K[z] is monic of degree 3. The map π may then be written as
π(x, y) = (Z(x, y), W(x, y)),
where Z and W are rational functions over K.
Remark 2.2. Using the relation y2 = F(x), we may write both Z and W as polynomials
in y of degree at most 1 (with coefficients that are rational functions in x). They satisfy
W 2 = f(Z) as functions on the curve X.
Remark 2.3. Let i denote the hyperelliptic involution on X, given in affine coordinates
by i(x, y) = (x, −y). Then the map π + π ◦i factors through the quotient X/i ∼= P1.
Since there is no nonconstant morphism from P1 to E, this map must be constant. After
changing the origin on E (or equivalently, post-composing with a translation on E), we
may therefore assume that it is constantly equal to the identity of E, that is, π ◦i = −π.
In terms of the affine representation π(x, y) = (Z(x, y), W(x, y)), this means that Z and
W/y are rational functions of x. In the following we thus write
π(x, y) = (Z(x), yW(x)).
Remark 2.4. Whenever necessary, we may assume that π: X →E is primitive, that
is, it does not admit any proper intermediate subcover. One can always reduce to this
case: factoring X →E as X →˜E →E with X →˜E primitive amounts to finding the
intermediate subfields between K(E) and K(X), which can be done by Galois theory. In
the primitive case, there is a complementary map π′ : X →E′ of degree equal to deg π
[5, p. 155]; this is the map we will determine.
FINDING THE COMPLEMENT OF AN ELLIPTIC CURVE INSIDE A JACOBIAN
3
2.1. Coordinates on X(2). Recall that the symmetric square X(2) of X is by definition
the quotient of X2 by the involution (p1, p2) 7→(p2, p1). Most of our calculations will
take place in X(2), so we now describe its function field. The function field K(X2) =
K(x1, x2, y1, y2) is the algebraic extension of K(x1, x2) with relations y2
1 = F(x1) and
y2
2 = F(x2). It is a Galois extension of K(x1 + x2, x1x2) with group isomorphic to the
dihedral group D4, generated by
r:

(x1, x2)
7→
(x2, x1),
(y1, y2)
7→
(y2, −y1),
s:

(x1, x2)
7→
(x2, x1),
(y1, y2)
7→
(y2, y1).
The field K(X(2)) is the subfield of K(X2) fixed by s. In particular,
K(X(2)) = K(x1 + x2, x1x2, y1 + y2, y1y2).
Notation 2.5. We will use the four functions
sx := x1 + x2,
px := x1x2,
sy := y1 + y2,
py := y1y2
as our system of coordinates on X(2).
2.2. The Jacobian of X. We view the Jacobian J = Jac X as birationally equivalent
to X(2). Note that X(2) may be identified with the set of effective divisors of degree 2
on X. Thus a point of X(2) may be written as (p1) + (p2), and corresponds to the class
[(p1) + (p2) −2(p0)] ∈J, where [·] denotes linear equivalence of divisors and p0 ∈X
is a fixed base point. We may embed X into X(2) by p 7−→(p) + (p0), and into J by
x 7−→[(p) −(p0)].
Remark 2.6. Notice that the map X(2) →J need not be bijective: the preimage of a
divisor class [D] ∈J consists of all pairs (p1) + (p2) ∈X(2) such that (p1) + (p2) −2(p0)
is linearly equivalent to D. This preimage is naturally identified with the complete linear
system |D + 2(p0)|, which is generically a single point (when dim |D + 2(p0)| = 0) and
isomorphic to P1 along a closed subvariety of J (where dim |D + 2(p0)| = 1) [18, §5].
Since two distinct degree-2 effective divisors on a genus 2 curve are linearly equivalent
only when they are in the canonical class, the morphism X(2) →J is the blow-up at the
point [KX −2(p0)], where [KX] is the canonical class. If we take p0 to be a Weierstrass
point, [KX −2(p0)] is simply the origin of J.
One may also describe the group law on J in these terms: for generic points, it is
expressed by linear equivalence of divisors, see for instance [22].
2.3. A model for the complementary curve E′. Our approach is based on the
following observation.
Proposition 2.7. Let π : X →E be a primitive cover. For c ∈E a given point, consider
the curve Φc in X(2) defined by the equation
π(p1) + π(p2) = c,
where p1, p2 ∈X. The complementary curve E′ may be obtained as a smooth model of
the unique irreducible component with positive genus. Moreover, for all but finitely many
c, there is a unique component.
Proof. Since J is a blow-down of X(2) contracting a single copy of P1 (Remark 2.6), the
curve Φc is birational to a fiber of the map π∗: J →E (specifically, the one over the
point c −2π(p0)). Note that ker π∗⊆J is connected since π is primitive [5, p. 154].
□
Remark 2.8. When π is not primitive, E′ is birational to any positive-genus component
of the curve π(p1) + π(p2) = c.
Remark 2.9. The fiber over c = 0 is given by π(p1) = −π(p2), so in the standard form of
Remark 2.3, this translates into the equations Z(x1) = Z(x2) and y1W(x1)+y2W(x2) =
0. The first equation is divisible by (x1 −x2), and this factor cuts out a component of Φ0
isomorphic to P1 that maps to a point in E′ ⊆J. This is the only genus-0 component of
4
GALLESE, LOMBARDO, NACCARATO, AND ZANNIER
Φ0, provided that π is primitive [7, §3]. The equations for the complementary curve ˜Φ0
are thus
(1)
Z(x1) −Z(x2)
x1 −x2
= 0
and
y1W(x1) + y2W(x2) = 0
in K(X(2)).
In particular, these equations define an irreducible curve.
2.4. The algorithm.
Algorithm 2.10. Given a primitive cover X →E, proceed as follows.
Step 1. Compute functions Z(x) and W(x) as in Remark 2.3.
Step 2. Express the equations for the complementary curve ˜Φ0 in X(2) given in Re-
mark 2.9 in terms of the symmetric functions x1 + x2, x1x2, y1 + y2, y1y2.
Step 3. Compute a smooth curve E′ birational to ˜Φ0 from these equations.
Step 4. Compute the complementary map π′ (Algorithm 2.20).
Remark 2.11. This algorithm computes a subvariety E′ of J up to birational equiva-
lence. The construction will show that E′ is also isomorphic to the quotient J/π∗E.
We now describe each of these steps in greater detail.
2.4.1. Step 1. Take an arbitrary point p0 ∈X. We compute q0 := π(p0) + π(i(p0)) ∈E
and a point q1 ∈E such that 2q1 = q0. Replacing π with τ−q1◦π, where τ−q1 is translation
by −q1, we may ensure that π + π ◦i is constantly equal to 0 ∈E. By Remark 2.3, π is
then of the form (x, y) 7→(Z(x), yW(x)).
2.4.2. Step 2. Remark 2.9 gives the equations of the complementary genus-1 curve ˜Φ0
inside X(2). These equations are expressed in the variables x1, x2, y1, y2 and are fixed
by the Galois automorphism s, so they belong to the subfield K(X(2)). Thus, the only
computational task to be solved in Step 2 is the following: given an element g of the field
K(X2) = K(x1, x2, y1, y2), explicitly represented as a rational function in x1, x2, y1, y2
and known to lie in the subfield K(X(2)), express g as a rational function of sx, px, sy, py.
Note that elements of K(X2) may be uniquely represented as K(x1, x2)-linear combina-
tions of 1, y1, y2, y1y2.
Decomposing g along this basis and using its stability under the Galois automor-
phism s, we may write g = g0(x1, x2) + g1(x1, x2) y1 + g1(x2, x1) y2 + g2(x1, x2) y1y2 with
g0(x1, x2) and g2(x1, x2) symmetric in their two variables. By taking iterated traces in
function fields, one finds the identity
(2)
g1(x1, x2) y1 + g1(x2, x1) y2 = h1(x1, x2)(y1 + y2) −h2(x1, x2) (y1y2)(y1 + y2),
where the functions
h1(x1, x2) = g1(x1, x2) F(x1) −g1(x2, x1) F(x2)
F(x1) −F(x2)
,
h2(x1, x2) = g1(x1, x2) −g1(x2, x1)
F(x1) −F(x2)
are symmetric in x1, x2. This reduces our problem to representing the symmetric func-
tions g0, g2, h1, h2 as rational functions of x1 + x2, x1x2. This may be done by standard
methods.
2.4.3. Step 3. Since F(x1) + F(x2) and F(x1)F(x2) are symmetric functions in x1, x2,
there exist computable rational functions Fsym,+ and Fsym,· such that
F(x1) + F(x2) = Fsym,+(sx, px),
F(x1)F(x2) = Fsym,·(sx, px).
The function field of X(2) is generated by sx, px, sy, py, subject to the relations s2
y =
y2
1 + 2y1y2 + y2
2 = F(x1) + F(x2) + 2py and p2
y = y2
1y2
2 = F(x1)F(x2), that is,
py = s2
y −Fsym,+(sx, px)
2
,
(3)
p2
y = Fsym,·(sx, px).
(4)
FINDING THE COMPLEMENT OF AN ELLIPTIC CURVE INSIDE A JACOBIAN
5
Thus, our model for ˜Φ0 is cut out in the four-dimensional affine space in the variables
sx, px, sy, py by equations (3) and (4), together with
Z(x1) −Z(x2)
x1 −x2
= 0,
(5)
y1W(x1) + y2W(x2) = 0.
(6)
By Step 2, we know how to express (5) and (6) in terms of sx, px, sy, py. Next, we show
that the curve defined in the (sx, px)-plane by (5) has geometric genus 0.
Remark 2.12. Although we write (5) and (6) as functions in x1, x2, y1, y2, one should
see them as functions in sx, px, sy, py. Formally, one starts with the variety defined by
(5) and (6) in X2 and considers its image in X(2).
Similarly, we may start with the curve defined by equation (5) in (P1)2 and consider
its image in the symmetric quotient (P1)(2). We denote this curve by T. This is the
object we informally referred to as the curve defined by (5) in the (sx, px)-plane.
Lemma 2.13. The curve T has geometric genus 0.
Proof. We denote by j the involution of X(2) given by
j
␀(p1) + (p2)

= (i(p1)) + (i(p2)).
There is a map ˜Φ0 →T induced by k[sx, px] ,→k[sx, px, sy, py].
This is clearly
dominant and j-invariant. It follows that T is dominated by ˜Φ0/⟨j⟩.
Under the birational identification X(2) →JX, taking, without loss of generality, the
base point p0 to be a Weierstrass point of X, the involution j corresponds to multipli-
cation by −1 on J. Since ˜Φ0 is birational to E′, we have ˜Φ0/⟨j⟩∼bir E′/[−1] ∼= P1. In
particular, T is dominated by the genus 0 curve ˜Φ0/⟨j⟩and therefore has genus 0.
□
Remark 2.14. The polynomials Z(x) for which the curve Z(x1)−Z(x2)
x1−x2
= 0 has a com-
ponent of genus 0 have been classified in [1]. Note however that, as already pointed out,
we do not directly work with (5), but rather its degree-2 quotient T, so all we can say in
our setting is that the curve in the (x1, x2)-plane defined by (5) is hyperelliptic (usually
of positive genus: see for example Lemma 3.1). Note furthermore that Z(x) is usually
a rational function and not a polynomial: it is a polynomial only if all the points in
π−1(0E) are points at infinity on X.
Since the curve T deduced from (5) is of geometric genus 0, we can parametrize sx, px
in terms of a new variable t. Moreover, by (3), the variable py is determined by the
others. Eliminating sx, px, py in favor of t and sy in (4) and (6) leaves us with two
(reducible) curves in the (t, sy)-plane. The only common component, which we may find
by computing the GCD of the defining polynomials, gives a model for ˜Φ0.
Remark 2.15. This model is of the form s2
y = q(t) for some rational function q(t).
To see this, note first that the identities in Step 2 show that (6) may be written as
(H1(sx, px) + H2(sx, px) py) sy = 0 for suitable functions H1, H2. The factor sy = 0 does
not appear in (4), so when taking the GCD of the two remaining equations we obtain
a divisor of H1(sx, px) + H2(sx, px) py, which is in particular linear in py. Using (3) to
eliminate py, we obtain a polynomial of the form H′
1(sx, px)s2
y −H′
2(sx, px), and finally
replacing sx, px with their expressions in terms of t leads to s2
y = H′
1(sx(t),px(t))
H′
2(sx(t),px(t)) =: q(t),
as claimed.
It only remains to compute the normalization of the curve s2
y = q(t), which is easy,
because we simply have to replace q(t) with the product of the squarefree parts of its
numerator and denominator.
2.4.4. Step 4. It remains to compute the complementary map π′ : X →E′. The next
proposition describes explicitly the natural map X ,→J →J/π∗E ∼= E′.
6
GALLESE, LOMBARDO, NACCARATO, AND ZANNIER
Proposition 2.16. Fix c = 0 to be the origin of E, and identify E′ with the normaliza-
tion of the genus-1 component ˜Φ0 of Φ0. Choose any origin on E′ to make it an elliptic
curve. A non-constant map π′ : X →E′ is given by
π′(p) =
X
q∈X
π(q)+π(p)=0
q̸=i(p)
((p) + (q))
−
X
q′∈X
π(q′)+π(p0)=0
q′̸=i(p0)
((p0) + (q′)),
where every pair (p) + (q) represents a point in ˜Φ0 ⊆X(2) and the sum is induced by the
elliptic curve operation on E′.
Remark 2.17. The advantage of this statement over the abstract composition X ,→
J →J/π∗E ∼= E′ is that the description of Proposition 2.16 allows us to only take sums
with respect to the elliptic curve structure on E′, rather than the more complicated group
structure on J. Also note that, in the above sums, points q in π−1(−π(p)) and points q′
in π−1(−π(p0)) should be repeated according to their multiplicity.
Remark 2.18. In the statement of the proposition, we fix an arbitrary origin on E′.
Since the two divisors being subtracted have the same degree, the resulting point of
Pic0(E′) is independent of this auxiliary choice.
Proof. Through the natural embedding X ,→X(2) and the birational morphism X(2) →
J, a point p ∈X is sent to
(7)
p 7→(p) + (p0) 7→(p) −(p0)
which lies on the fiber Φa with a = π(p) + π(p0). To get a point on the fixed fiber Φ0,
we compose (7) with
(8)
(p) −(p0) 7→deg(π) · [(p) −(p0)] −π∗π∗[(p) −(p0)].
The point on the right-hand side of (8) lies in ker π∗, which we identify birationally with
the genus-1 component ˜Φ0 of Φ0. Moreover, if the resulting map X →Φ0 were constant
(hence constantly equal to 0), every point of X ⊆J would lie in [deg π]−1 (π∗E) ⊆J.
Since each irreducible component of [deg π]−1 (π∗E) is isomorphic to π∗E ∼= E, this would
force X to be contained in an elliptic curve, contradiction. Expanding the definition of
pull-back and push-forward, we rewrite the right-hand side of (8) as
deg(π)(p) −deg(π)(p0) −
X
q∈X
π(q)=π(p)
(q)
+
X
q′∈X
π(q′)=π(p0)
(q′).
Let κ be a canonical divisor on X. It is well known that (q) ∼κ −(i(q)) for every
q ∈X; using this for all q (resp. q′) that appear in the sum, and cancelling out all the
occurrences of κ (note that #{q : π(q) = π(p)} = #{q′ : π(q′) = π(p0)} = deg π, where
the points are counted with multiplicity), we obtain
deg(π)(p) −deg(π)(p0) +
X
q∈X
π(q)+π(p)=0
(q)
−
X
q′∈X
π(q′)+π(p0)=0
(q′).
Reorganizing, one gets the sum in the statement. The divisor (p)+(i(p))−(p0)−(i(p0))
is linearly equivalent to zero and therefore (p) + (i(p)), (p0) + (i(p0)) may be removed
from the sums (one can show that (q) + (i(q)) lies in the genus-0 component of Φ0).
□
We may compute the image of any point on the genus-2 curve X under the map
π′ : X →E′ using Proposition 2.16. One can avoid the computation of the individual
points q in π−1(−π(p)) by describing the full divisor
(9)
Γp :=
X
q∈X
π(q)+π(p)=0
q̸=i(p)
((p) + (q))
on the curve ˜Φ0 as the vanishing locus of polynomials with coefficients in the field of
definition of p.
FINDING THE COMPLEMENT OF AN ELLIPTIC CURVE INSIDE A JACOBIAN
7
Remark 2.19. Once the divisors Γp, Γp0 are known, we obtain π′(p) by summing the
points in the support of Γp −Γp0 using the (birational) group law on ˜Φ0 (which we may
compute by mapping the divisor to E′ and summing there, or by suitably applying the
Riemann-Roch theorem, which avoids the computation of the points in its support).
We now give equations for (9). Note that the conditions π(q) + π(p) = 0, q ̸= i(p)
simply amount to (p)+(q) being a point on ˜Φ0. Thus, (9) is the divisor on ˜Φ0 consisting
of those D = (p1) + (p2), seen as effective divisors of degree 2, where one of the two
points p1, p2 in the support is the fixed point p. The set of divisors (p) + (p2) in X(2) for
which p is one of the two points in the support is the image in X(2) of
(10)
{p} × X ∪X × {p} ⊂X2.
The subvariety cut out in X2 by the (symmetric) equations
(11)
(
(x1 −x(p))(x2 −x(p)) = 0
(y1 −y(p))(y2 −y(p)) = 0
differs from (10) only by a finite number of points: a point (p1, p2) that satisfies the first
equation has either x1 = x(p) or x2 = x(p). By symmetry, suppose we are in the first
case. Then either y1 = y(p), in which case we have p1 = p and hence (p1, p2) ∈{p} × X,
or y1 ̸= y(p), which forces y1 = −y(p) and y2 = y(p), which in turn gives that x2 satisfies
F(x2) = y2
2 = y(p)2, giving only a finite number of points. Thus, for generic p, the
projection of (11) to X(2) will meet ˜Φ0 precisely in Γp. It is also easy to test if this
condition is satisfied for a specific p, since this happens if and only if the intersection
consists of precisely deg Γp = deg π −1 points (counted with multiplicity).
Proceeding as in Step 2, we may write down functions on X(2) that cut out the locus
described by (11). Intersecting with ˜Φ0 gives the desired divisor (9). This leads to the
following algorithm for the computation of π′.
Algorithm 2.20. Given a primitive cover π : X →E,
(1) Compute ˜Φ0, a smooth model E′ : (w′)2 = h(z′), and the birational map ˜Φ0 99K
E′.
(2) Compute the divisors Γi(p0) and Γp0 on ˜Φ0. Identify them with divisors on E′
via the birational map of step (1). Let q0 be the point on E′ corresponding to the
divisor class [Γi(p0) −Γp0] ∈Jac(E′), and let q1 ∈E′ be such that q0 = 2q1.
(3) The map τ−q1 ◦π′ is computable and, by Remark 2.3, of the form (Z′(x), yW ′(x)).
Compute (Z′(x), yW ′(x)) for 2 deg π + 1 points (x, y) ∈X.
(4) Recover the rational function Z′(x) by interpolation over these images.
(5) Deduce W ′(x) = ±
p
h(Z′(x))/F(x).
Remark 2.21. In Step (2), it may happen that some points in the support of Γp0 or
Γi(p0) lie in the (finite) indeterminacy locus of the birational map ˜Φ0 →E′. If this is the
case, one may simply use a different base point p0.
Remark 2.22. Alternatively, we could replace steps (3)-(5) with a single computation
for a generic point of X, but this seems harder to implement. Also note that in Step (5)
the sign may be fixed by comparison with one of the values computed in Step (3) – using
any such value for which yW ′(x) ̸= 0.
Proof. We have already justified the correctness and computability of all the steps except
(5). Note that the degree of Z′ as a rational function in x is equal to deg π′, which in
turn is equal to deg π since π is primitive [5, p. 155]. A degree-n rational function may
be interpolated from its values at 2n + 1 points.
□
3. Motivation
3.1. Higher dimensional split Jacobians. The following natural question remains
open: let π: X →X1 be a ramified cover such that Jac X splits, up to isogeny, as a
product of Jacobians of curves X1, X2, . . . , Xr. How can one explicitly recover the curves
Xi occurring in this decomposition?
8
GALLESE, LOMBARDO, NACCARATO, AND ZANNIER
Work of Lombardo, Lorenzo García, Ritzenthaler, and Sijsling [15] sug-
gests that the Xi can be recovered as quotients of the Galois closure of the composition
X →X1 →P1 for a suitably chosen low-degree map X1 →P1.
This strategy has
been formalized into a conjecture and established in the case g(X) = 2, g(X1) = 1 by
Gallese [7], via an explicit construction of the complementary elliptic curve E′ = X2.
That construction has since been revised to align with the one presented in this note,
substantially simplifying the arguments in the first version of [7].
3.2. Critical values of polynomials. A different motivation arises from the third au-
thor’s study of critical elliptic curves [20, §2]. These are, up to a twist, elliptic curves
E/K with a double cover E →P1 that branches over the same four places as the cover
g : P1 →P1 induced by a quartic polynomial g ∈K[x]. More specifically, given any quar-
tic polynomial g, Naccarato defines the critical curve relative to g as the projective
cubic with affine model
Eg : w2 = Discx(g(x) −z).
(12)
Here we assume char(K) = 0. We remark that (12) defines an elliptic curve if and only if
the j-invariant of the ramification points—that is, that of u2 = g′(x)—is different from
1728 and ∞(see [19, §4.2]), which we assume from now on. The study of critical elliptic
curves plays a central role in understanding the arithmetic of critical values (i.e. branch
points) of quartic polynomials; more details can be found in [19, §5] and [20].
The fiber product
(13)
Cg = P1
x ×
P1z
Eg
has the affine model
(14)
y2 = F(x) := Disct
g(t) −g(x)
t −x

.
Indeed, let ∆(z) = Disct(g(t) −z); (13) gives the equation
w2 = ∆(g(x))
(15)
for Cg. Noticing that
∆(g(x)) = Disct(g(t)−g(x)) = Disct

(t −x)g(t) −g(x)
t −x

= g′(x)2 Disct
g(t) −g(x)
t −x

and setting y = (g′(x))−1w transforms (15) to y2 = F(x). This model is smooth: to
show this, it suffices to prove that F(x) has six distinct roots. Let c1, c2, c3 be the three
(distinct, by our previous assumption) finite branch points of g. The roots of F are
the g-preimages of its finite branch points where g does not ramify, that is, the points
{x ∈g−1(ci)
 g′(x) ̸= 0}. As each root of g′ has to map with ramification index 2 to a
distinct branch point (a higher index would correspond to a double root of g′, preventing
it from having two more roots), we get a total of 2 + 2 + 2 = 6 distinct roots for F.
Observe that, by construction, there is a map πg : Cg →Eg given by:
(x, y) 7→(g(x), yg′(x)).
(16)
A peculiarity of the quartic polynomial case is that one can drop the second equation
from (1), once (5) is considered in K(X2) (rather than in the symmetric square):
Lemma 3.1. Let g ∈K[x] be a quartic polynomial such that (12) defines an elliptic
curve, and let
X = Cg,
E = Eg,
π = πg,
so that Z(x) = g(x). Then, a plane affine model for E′ is given by
Z(u) −Z(v)
u −v
= 0.
(17)
FINDING THE COMPLEMENT OF AN ELLIPTIC CURVE INSIDE A JACOBIAN
9
Proof. Let L be the splitting field of g(x)−z over K(z), the roots being x = x1, x2, x3, x4,
and let D be the normalization of the plane curve defined by (17). It is well-known
[23, §4.4] that if Eg is an elliptic curve—the case of Morse polynomials—then G =
Gal(L/K(z)) ≃S4. Sending (u, v) to (x1, x2) gives an isomorphism between K(D) and
L⟨(34)⟩. In [20, §4.1] it is shown that D has genus 1.
For each j ∈{1, 2, 3, 4} we define the L-point
Pj =

xj,
w
g′(xj)

∈X(L),
which satisfies π(Pj) = (z, w); notice that w = yg′(x1). We also introduce the degree
2 effective divisor H = P3 + i(P4) ∈X(2)(L). Since, for σ ∈G, σ(w) = sgn(σ)w, one
checks easily that StabG(H) = ⟨(34)⟩. It follows that H descends to an effective divisor
of degree 2 with coordinates in L⟨(34)⟩∼= K(D), that is, a point in X(2)(K(D)). By
definition, this is the same as a rational map D 99K X(2). Composing with the natural
map X(2) →J given by (p1) + (p2) 7→[(p1) + (p2) −KX], we finally get a rational map
Ψ : D 99K J
which, since D is a smooth curve and J is projective, can in fact be extended to a
morphism. By construction, the generic point of D maps to η := [(P3) + (i(P4)) −KX].
Applying π∗to this point we find π∗(η) = [(z, w)+(z, −w)−π∗(KX)] = 0, so the generic
point of D maps inside ker π∗, and therefore Ψ(D) ⊂E′ .
To conclude that Ψ induces a birational morphism Ψ : D →E′, we just need to show
that deg Ψ = 1. Two degree 2 effective divisors on a genus 2 curve are linearly equivalent
only when they are in the canonical class, so on the open set x3 ̸= x4 we have injectivity,
and we are done.
□
Remark 3.2. Let Y be a curve with function field L. The proof of Lemma 3.1 constructs
the following diagram:
Y
{1}
X
D
⟨(234)⟩
⟨(34)⟩
E
P1
x
A4
Stab(1)
P1
z
S4
3
2
2
4
3
12
2
4
Note that the extension L/K(P1
x) is the splitting field of the cubic polynomial (g(t) −
g(x))/(t −x). The elliptic curve E corresponds by definition to the quadratic extension
of K(z) generated by the square root of the discriminant of g(x) −z, and hence to the
index-2 subgroup A4 of S4. The fiber product X thus corresponds to the intersection of
A4 and the stabilizer of 1 in S4, namely, the group generated by (234). This is the copy of
A3 inside the group S3 ∼= Gal(Y/P1
x). Hence, X corresponds to the quadratic extension of
K(x) generated by the square root of the discriminant of (g(t) −g(x))/(t −x): this gives
another proof that a model for X is given by y2 = F(x). Also note that π is primitive,
since ⟨(234)⟩is not contained in any larger proper subgroup of A4. Furthermore, one can
prove that Ψ: D →J is induced by the correspondence Y between X and D [7, §2].
Computing a model for the complementary curve E′
g in Jac(Cg) has, for instance, the
following application [20, Theorem 2]:
Corollary 3.3. Let g be a quartic polynomial with rational coefficients. The equation
g(u) = g(t),
u ̸= t
has infinitely many solutions over Q(i), unless the j-invariant of the ramification points
of g is 0, −27648/11 or 55296/5.
10
GALLESE, LOMBARDO, NACCARATO, AND ZANNIER
4. Examples
Example 4.1 (Degree 3). Kuhn [10, §6] provides an explicit presentation of the moduli
space of degree-3 covers π: X →E in terms of three parameters a, b, c. Taking (a, b, c) =
(3, 4, 5) yields the cover between the curves
X : y2 = x6 + 19
5 x5 + 42
5 x4 + 309
20 x3 + 63
4 x2 + 15x + 25
4 =: F(x),
E : −20
247w2 = z3 −84
247z2 + 164
247z −20
247,
given by (x, y) 7→(Z(x), yW(x)) with
Z(x) :=
x2
x3 + 3x2 + 4x + 5,
W(x) :=
x3 −4x −10
x6 + 6x5 + 17x4 + 34x3 + 46x2 + 40x + 25.
In this case, once expressed in terms of sx, px, equation (5) becomes
(18)
sx −1
5p2
x + 4
5px = 0,
which is clearly a curve of genus 0 that we may parametrize using px. One can show in
general that, for every degree-3 map π, the resulting equation (5) is linear in sx. Once
we eliminate sx and py using (18) and (3), and take the GCD of the remaining equations
in the variables (px, sy), we are left with the model
56s2
y = px(p3
x −4p2
x + 15px −25)(p4
x −10p3
x + 32p2
x −189
2 px + 150)2,
which has the form described in Remark 2.15. The normalization step is trivial, since
it consists only of reabsorbing the squares, so we obtain the smooth genus one curve
y2 = x(x3 −4x2 + 15x −25), with Weierstrass model
(19)
E′ : y2 = x3 + 25x + 375.
We verify that the j-invariant of E′ agrees with Kuhn’s computations, and confirm the
same for a sample of further parameter triples (a, b, c). Applying Algorithm 2.20, we find
the explicit degree-3 map π′ : X →E′ given by
π′(x, y) =
 
−6x3 −6x2 −35/4x + 25/4
x3 + 4/5x2 + 2x + 5/4
, −3x3 + 55/2x2 + 25/2x + 125/8
(x3 + 4/5x2 + 2x + 5/4)2
y
!
.
As predicted by [10], the denominators of the x-coordinates of the maps π and π′ satisfy
(x3+3x2+4x+5)(x3+4/5x2+2x+5/4) = F(x). We refer to [8] for the full computational
details.
Example 4.2 (Degree 4). Taking as Z(x) the quartic polynomial g(x) = x4 −8x2 +16x,
(12) and (14) give models
Eg : −w2 = z3 + 32z2 −896z + 4864,
Cg : −y2 = x6 −16x4 + 40x3 + 80x2 −288x + 304
for the curves of §3.2, after rescaling w and y. Exploiting Lemma 3.1, we can compute
the Weierstrass model
(w′)2 = (z′)3 −432z′ −8208
for E′
g.
This is the quadratic twist by −1 of the elliptic curve with Cremona label
11a3 [13], the so-called first elliptic curve in nature. Notice that (E′
g)(−1)(Q) ≃Z/5Z is
finite: this is one of the exceptional cases of Corollary 3.3, as one can check by verifying
that the j-invariant of u2 = g′(x) is −27648
11 . Indeed, generically we have rk(E′
g)(−1)(Q) >
0, which is the key fact behind the infinitude result given in the corollary, see [20, §4].
Example 4.3 (Degree 5). We perform a computation analogous to that of Example 4.1,
starting from Shaska’s presentation [24] of the moduli space of degree-5 covers π: X →
E in terms of two parameters a, b. We take (a, b) = (7, 1). Any model for X is defined
over an extension of Q of degree at least 2; we take as base field the quadratic number
field Q(z), where z2 + z −5 = 0. Explicit models for E, X, and the map π can be found
by running the code in the accompanying repository [8].
FINDING THE COMPLEMENT OF AN ELLIPTIC CURVE INSIDE A JACOBIAN
11
The main difference from the degree-3 case arises in Equation (5), which gives
s4
x + 130s3
xpx + 130s3
x + 4255s2
xp2
x −33728s2
xpx + 4255s2
x
+ 19500sxp3
x + 114140sxp2
x + 114140sxpx + 19500sx
+ 225p4
x −707130p3
x + 14336251p2
x −707130px + 225 = 0.
This again defines a curve of geometric genus 0, which, unlike the degree-3 case, admits
no non-singular point over Q(z), so producing a parametrization requires a further field
extension. Carrying this out and substituting into the remaining equations, one obtains
a singular model for E′ whose resolution yields a Weierstrass equation with the same
j-invariant as one can obtain from the formulas in [24].
5. Further comments
5.1. On the j-invariants of E, E′. Assume π is primitive of degree n. Then Jac X is
isogenous to (E × E′)/Γψ, where ψ : E[n]
∼
−→E′[n] is a Galois-equivariant anti-isometry
for the Weil pairing [5, 9] and Γψ is its graph. If E, E′ are non-isogenous and K is large
enough (e.g. contains E[n], E′[n]), such ψ exist, and one can realize any such pair via
a genus-2 curve with degree-n maps [5, Proposition 1.5]. In particular, we recover the
well-known fact that, even for fixed n, there is no universal equation Pn(j(E), j(E′)) = 0
satisfied by the j-invariants of E and E′. We give a simple example that demonstrates
this explicitly for n = 2.
Example 5.1. Let n = 2. For sufficiently general j, j′ ∈K, we may find Weierstrass
equations
E : y2 = x3 + ax2 + bx + 1
and
E′ : y2 = x3 + bx2 + ax + 1
with j(E) = j, j(E′) = j′. Then
X : y2 = x6 + ax4 + bx2 + 1
admits degree-2 maps (x, y) 7→(x2, y) and (x, y) 7→(x−2, yx−3) to E and E′, respectively.
Since generically any pair j(E), j(E′) may be realized in this way, the j-invariants of
E, E′ satisfy no algebraic relation.
Remark 5.2. One may still wonder about families of covers C →E determined by an
algebraic relation between the j-invariants of E and E′. A collection of such families is
provided by the modular polynomials [6, 4], which prescribe an isogeny between E and
E′. Naturally interesting families arise by prescribing the ramification behaviour of the
associated cover g : C/⟨i⟩→E/[−1]; for instance, in the context of Lemma 3.1, the
elliptic curves Eg and E′
g are generically non-isogenous, but, letting j be the j-invariant
of u2 = g′(x), we have:
j(E) = j(j −1536)3
218(j −1728),
j(E′) =
j2
4(j −1728).
For a proof of these facts, see [20, §5].
5.2. Connection with quadratic twists. Assume, as in Remark 2.3, that π ◦i = −π.
We may then write π(x, y) =
␀Z(x), yW(x)

with
F(x)W(x)2 = f(Z(x)).
Equivalently, P(t) =
␀Z(t), W(t)

is a point in E(F (t))(K(t)), where E(F (t)) : F(t)w2 =
f(z) denotes the quadratic twist of E by F(t) ∈K(t)×. Such a point P(t) is necessarily
non-torsion: over K(t,
p
F(t)) the twist is isomorphic to E via (z, w) 7→(z,
p
F(t) w),
and a torsion point would have coordinates that are constant in t, contradicting the non-
constancy of Z(t). Conversely, any nonconstant K(t)-point on E(F (t)) yields a surjective
map X →E. The complementary map π′ : X →E′ similarly yields a nonconstant point
P ′(t) on
E′(F (t)) : F(t)w′2 = h(z′).
12
GALLESE, LOMBARDO, NACCARATO, AND ZANNIER
Specializing at t = t0 ∈K with F(t0) ̸= 0 gives points P(t0), P ′(t0) on E(F (t0)) and
E′(F (t0)). When K is a number field, by Silverman’s specialization theorem [25], these
are non-torsion for t0 of large height, so we have proved that there exist infinitely many
d = F(t0) ∈K such that the quadratic twists E(d), E′(d) both have positive rank.
Faltings’s theorem implies that for each d ∈K× there are only finitely many solutions
(t0, y) to the equation dy2 = F(t0), so the d we have constructed give infinitely many
distinct classes in K×/K×2.
Remark 5.3. Stronger results are known: for instance, [12, Theorem 4] shows that,
for any pair of elliptic curves E1, E2 over Q that satisfy neither j(E1) = j(E2) = 0 nor
j(E1) = j(E2) = 1728, there exist infinitely many rational numbers d such that E(d)
1
and E(d)
2
both have positive rank. The proof relies on the consideration of the Kummer
surface f1(x1) = z2f2(x2), where Ei is defined by the equation y2 = fi(x). Similar ideas,
also related to the density of rational points on Kummer surfaces, appear in the appendix
of [3].
5.3. Generalizations. If π : X →E is a cover from a curve of arbitrary genus g to
an elliptic curve, we may proceed in a similar way to describe a complement of π∗E
inside J := Jac X.
Namely, we may birationally identify J with X(g), and describe
birationally the Prym variety of X →E (that is, Prym(X/E) := (ker π∗: J →E)0) as
an irreducible component of π(p1) + · · · + π(pg) = c for sufficiently general c ∈E. When
g(X) = 3, Prym(X/E) is generically isogenous to the Jacobian of a genus-2 curve Y , but
unfortunately, we do not know an easy way to recover a suitable Y from this construction
(see [21] and [15] for results in this direction).
Similarly, if π : Y →X is a cover between curves of genera g(Y ) = 3 and g(X) = 2,
one may use similar techniques to describe the elliptic curve E := Prym(Y/X) bira-
tionally inside Y (3). However, in this case, the Riemann-Hurwitz formula shows that π
is necessarily an unramified double cover, and there are easier ways to recover E (see for
example [2, Proposition 2.2]).

X(t), Y = Y (t). Compositio Math., 139(3):263–295, 2003.

braic varieties. Math. Z., 286(1-2):579–602, 2017.

Bordeaux, 37(1):49–77, 2025.

cation. In Arithmetic algebraic geometry (Texel, 1989), volume 89 of Progr. Math., pages 153–176.
Birkhäuser Boston, Boston, MA, 1991.
[6] Gerhard Frey and Ernst Kani. Curves of genus 2 with elliptic differentials and associated Hurwitz
spaces. In Arithmetic, geometry, cryptography and coding theory, volume 487 of Contemp. Math.,
pages 33–81. Amer. Math. Soc., Providence, RI, 2009.
[7] Andrea Gallese. How to split two-dimensional Jacobians: a geometric construction, 2025. https:
//arxiv.org/abs/2412.07414.
[8] Andrea Gallese, Davide Lombardo, Francesco Naccarato, and Umberto Zannier. GitHub repository.
https://github.com/G4ll/NoteOnGenus2Covers, 2026.

of curves of genus two or three. Forum Math., 12(3):315–364, 2000.

1988.
[11] Abhinav Kumar. Hilbert modular surfaces for square discriminants and elliptic subfields of genus 2
function fields. Res. Math. Sci., 2:Art. 24, 46, 2015.

Math. Res. Notices, (4):113–123, 1993.

Comp., 88(316):889–929, 2019.

Jacobians via Galois covers. Exp. Math., 32(1):218–240, 2023.
